title: वायु सूक्तम्
devanagariText:
॥ श्री वायु सूक्तम् ॥

ॐ वाय उक्थेभिर्जरन्ते त्वामच्छा जरितारः।
सुतसोमा अहर्विदः॥ १॥

वायो तव प्रपृञ्चती धेना जिगाति दाशुषे।
उरूची सोमपीतये॥ २॥

वायवा याहि दर्शतेमे सोमा अरंकृताः।
तेषां पाहि श्रुधी हवम्॥ ३॥

इन्द्रवायू इमे सुता उप प्रयोभिरा गतम्।
इन्दवो वामुशन्ति हि॥ ४॥

वायविन्द्रश्च चेतथः सुतानां वाजिनीवसू।
तावा याताम् उप द्रवत्॥ ५॥

वायविन्द्रश्च सुन्वत आ यातमुप निष्कृतम्।
मक्ष्वित्था धिया नरा॥ ६॥

वातायां धर्मणस्पते वातायां पतये नमः।
नमस्ते वायो त्वमेव प्रत्यक्षं ब्रह्मासि॥ ७॥

त्वामेव प्रत्यक्षं ब्रह्म वदिष्यामि।
ऋतं वदिष्यामि। सत्यं वदिष्यामि।
तन्मामवतु। तद्वक्तारमवतु।
अवतु माम्। अवतु वक्तारम्॥ ८॥

ॐ शान्तिः शान्तिः शान्तिः॥
transliteration:
|| Shri Vayu Suktam ||

Om Vaya ukthebhir-jarante tvam-achchha jaritarah |
Suta-soma ahar-vidah || 1 ||

Vayo tava praprinchati dhena jigati dashushe |
Uruchi soma-pitaye || 2 ||

Vayav-aa yahi darshate-me soma arankritah |
Tesham pahi shrudhi havam || 3 ||

Indra-Vayu ime suta upa prayobhir-aa gatam |
Indavo vam-ushanti hi || 4 ||

Vayav-Indrash-cha chetathaḥ sutanam vajini-vasu |
Tav-aa yatam upa dravat || 5 ||

Vayav-Indrash-cha sunvata aa yatam-upa nishkritam |
Makshv-ittha dhiya nara || 6 ||

Vatayam dharmanaspate Vatayam pataye namah |
Namaste Vayo tvam-eva pratyaksham Brahma-asi || 7 ||

Tvam-eva pratyaksham Brahma vadishyami |
Ritam vadishyami | Satyam vadishyami |
Tan-mam-avatu | Tad-vaktaram-avatu |
Avatu mam | Avatu vaktaram || 8 ||

Om Shantih Shantih Shantih ||
hindiMeaning:
हे वायु! स्तोता उक्थों (स्तुति-मन्त्रों) से आपकी ओर स्तुति करते हैं, सोम निचोड़ने वाले, दिन-ज्ञाता॥ १॥

हे वायु! आपकी वाणी-रूपी धेनु हविर्दाता के लिए जाती है, विस्तृत, सोमपान के लिए॥ २॥

हे दर्शनीय वायु! आइए, ये सोम सजाकर रखे हैं। इनका पान कीजिए, हमारी पुकार सुनिए॥ ३॥

हे इन्द्र और वायु! ये सोम निचोड़े गए हैं, प्रीतिकारक भोगों सहित आइए। सोम-रस आपको चाहते हैं॥ ४॥

हे वायु और इन्द्र! आप दोनों निचोड़े गए सोमों को जानते हैं, वाजिनीवसु! शीघ्र आइए॥ ५॥

हे वायु और इन्द्र! सोम निचोड़ने वाले के पास तैयार सोम के समीप आइए, शीघ्र, बुद्धि से, हे नर॥ ६॥

हे वायु, धर्म के स्वामी! वायु, पति को नमस्कार। हे वायु! आप ही प्रत्यक्ष ब्रह्म हैं॥ ७॥

आपको ही प्रत्यक्ष ब्रह्म कहूँगा। ऋत कहूँगा। सत्य कहूँगा। वह मेरी रक्षा करे। वह वक्ता की रक्षा करे॥ ८॥
benefits: Invokes Vayu, the god of wind and vital breath (Prana)
Enhances pranic energy and respiratory health
Vayu is declared as direct manifestation of Brahman (Pratyaksha Brahma)
Purifies the atmosphere and brings fresh life force
Invokes truth, righteousness, and spiritual clarity
Powerful for pranayama practitioners and yogis
Traditional Vedic hymn for vitality and life-breath
description: Dedicated to Lord Vayu, the Vayu Suktam (वायु सूक्तम्) is a Vedic hymn revered in Hindu tradition. Attributed to Rigveda (Mandala 1) / Taittiriya Upanishad, this prayer holds a special place in Hindu devotional literature. Devotees recite this prayer to enhance pranic energy and respiratory health and purify the atmosphere and bring fresh life force. It is also said to grant truth, righteousness, and spiritual clarity. This prayer is traditionally recited on Saturday (Shanivar) to invoke divine grace. At 8 verses long, the recitation takes roughly 5 minutes, making it accessible for regular devotional use. On this page, you can read the complete Vayu Suktam in Devanagari Sanskrit with English transliteration, Hindi meaning (arth), and free PDF download for offline recitation.

seoFaqs.question: What is the Vayu Suktam?
seoFaqs.answer: The Vayu Suktam (वायु सूक्तम्) is a short Vedic hymn dedicated to Vayu — the Vedic god of wind and the cosmic vital-breath (prana) that pervades all living beings. Verses 1-6 of the Suktam are drawn from Rigveda Mandala 1, Sukta 2 (the second hymn of the entire Rigveda — composed by Madhuchchhanda Vaishvamitra in Gayatri metre) where Vayu is invoked along with Indra to come and partake of the Soma offering. Verse 7-8 is from the Taittiriya Upanishad / Aruna Prashna shanti-mantra — Namaste Vayo tvameva pratyaksham Brahma-asi (Salutations Vayu, You alone are the directly-perceived Brahman) — making the Vayu Suktam one of the very few Vedic texts that explicitly identifies a deity with the impersonal Brahman of the Upanishads. The hymn is preserved in classical Vedic tradition. (Source: Rigveda Mandala 1 / Taittiriya Upanishad.)

seoFaqs.question: Why is Vayu called Pratyaksha Brahma (the directly-perceived Brahman)?
seoFaqs.answer: The Taittiriya Upanishad shanti-mantra declares Namaste Vayo tvameva pratyaksham Brahma-asi — You, O Vayu, are the directly-perceived Brahman. This is one of the most theologically significant statements in Vedic literature because: (1) Brahman is otherwise described as nirakara (formless), nirguna (attributeless) and beyond perception — but Vayu as wind/breath is felt directly by every living being on every breath; (2) prana (life-breath) is the closest experiential proof of consciousness — without breath there is no life, hence prana is identified with Brahman in the Prashna Upanishad and Chandogya Upanishad; (3) Vayu is the moving force behind speech, fire, body, mind, and senses — the antaryami (inner controller) of all beings; (4) in Madhva Vaishnava tradition, Vayu is even regarded as the chief jiva and avatara-takes are Hanuman, Bhima, and Madhvacharya himself. (Sources: Taittiriya Upanishad 1.1; Prashna Upanishad 2.13; Chandogya Upanishad 4.3.)

seoFaqs.question: What is the significance of the Vayav-Indra invocation in the Suktam?
seoFaqs.answer: Verses 4-6 of the Vayu Suktam jointly invoke Vayu and Indra — Vayav-Indrash-cha sunvata aa yatam (Vayu and Indra, come quickly to the Soma-presser). This twin-invocation is significant because: (1) Indra is the king of devas and warrior-deity, while Vayu is his charioteer and inseparable companion in Vedic mythology (similar to Krishna-Arjuna); (2) in the Soma yajna ritual, the very first Soma-cup (asvina-graha or vayava-graha) is offered to Vayu before Indra — making Vayu the prathama-pita (first drinker) of every Vedic sacrifice; (3) philosophically, Indra represents the conscious-mind (manas) and Vayu represents the life-breath (prana) — and together they constitute the inner instrument (antah-karana) of the jiva; (4) the joint invocation establishes the Vedic principle that all worship must integrate prana (Vayu) and chitta (Indra-mind) together. (Sources: Rigveda Mandala 1, Sukta 2; Aitareya Brahmana on Soma yajna.)

seoFaqs.question: How does Vayu Suktam connect to pranayama and yoga practice?
seoFaqs.answer: The Vayu Suktam is one of the foundational Vedic texts for pranayama practitioners and yoga sadhakas because: (1) Vayu in the body is the prana — the five vital-airs (Prana, Apana, Vyana, Udana, Samana) discussed in Patanjali Yoga Sutras 2.49-53 and Hatha Yoga Pradipika; (2) the Suktam invokes Vayu as Dharmanaspati (Lord of Dharma — Yama-Niyama) and Vata-pati (Lord of Vata — the dosha governing nervous system and movement in Ayurveda); (3) chanting the Suktam before pranayama practice is held to invoke Vayu's blessing for steady breath, deep inhalation, and conscious exhalation; (4) the final shanti mantra — Avatu mam, Avatu vaktaram (May He protect me, May He protect the speaker) — is recited as the opening shanti-patha of every Krishna Yajurveda Upanishad including the Taittiriya, Mahanarayana, Katha, and Shvetashvatara Upanishads. The Suktam is thus the foundational Vedic invocation for breath-based sadhana. (Sources: Patanjali Yoga Sutras; Hatha Yoga Pradipika; Krishna Yajurveda shanti patha.)

seoFaqs.question: When should the Vayu Suktam be recited for maximum benefit?
seoFaqs.answer: The Vayu Suktam can be recited daily as part of Sandhya Vandana, but holds special significance on Saturday (Shanivar — when Hanuman the chief Vayu-avatara is also worshipped), every Tuesday (Hanuman Jayanti day in many traditions), every Ekadashi, Hanuman Jayanti (Chaitra Purnima or Margashirsha Krishna Chaturdashi), Madhva Navami (when Madhvacharya — third Vayu avatara — is honored), Bhima Jayanti, before any pranayama or yoga session, before yajna or homa rituals (since Vayu is the messenger between humans and devas), during respiratory illness as Vedic remedy chant, and as the opening invocation of any Krishna Yajurveda Upanishad parayana. South Indian Madhva Brahmins recite it as part of the daily Vayu-stuti before Vishnu-puja. (Sources: classical Sandhya Vandana paddhati; Madhva Vaishnava daily ritual.)

seoFaqs.question: Who composed the Vayu Suktam and what is its Vedic source?
seoFaqs.answer: The first six verses of the Vayu Suktam are from Rigveda Mandala 1, Sukta 2 — composed by the Vedic rishi Madhuchchhanda Vaishvamitra (son of Vishvamitra) in the Gayatri metre. This is the second hymn of the entire Rigveda — placed immediately after the famous Agni Sukta (Agnimile Purohitam) — making it among the oldest preserved religious compositions in human history (estimated 1500-1200 BCE). Verses 7-8 are from the Taittiriya Upanishad shanti-mantra (Krishna Yajurveda) — preserved in the Aruna Prashna and recited as the opening invocation of multiple Upanishads. The text has been continuously chanted for over 3000 years across Vedic shakhas (Shakala-shakha for Rigveda, Taittiriya-shakha for Yajurveda) and is considered apaurusheya (not of human authorship — eternal revelation). (Sources: Rigveda Samhita Mandala 1; Taittiriya Upanishad shanti patha.)
